g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Java Maps - the essential key-value data structure for efficient data retrieval.
A Map stores key-value pairs where each unique key maps to exactly one value - like a dictionary lookup.
g.text: Map<K,V> stores key-value pairs where each
unique key maps to ONE value.

g.data-narration: Here's how to create a HashMap and add/retrieve values using put() and get() methods.
HashMap uses hash table implementation providing O(1) average time for put and get operations.
LinkedHashMap maintains insertion order - elements are stored in the order they were added.
TreeMap uses Red-Black tree ensuring keys are kept in sorted (natural) order with O(log n) operations.
Hashtable is a legacy synchronized class - thread-safe but doesn't allow null keys or values.
Different Map implementations handle null keys and values differently - know which allows what!
Core Map operations include put, get, containsKey, remove, and size for managing key-value pairs.
Maps provide three collection views: keySet() for all keys, values() for all values, and entrySet() for key-value pairs.
The best way to iterate a Map is using entrySet() with a for-each loop - access both key and value.
HashMap uses hash function to compute bucket index. Collisions are handled via chaining (linked lists or trees).
HashMap offers O(1) for basic operations, TreeMap provides O(log n) with sorted keys, LinkedHashMap adds ordering with O(1).
Maps are used for caching, frequency counting, lookup tables, and configuration management in real applications.
Remember: HashMap for speed, LinkedHashMap for insertion order, TreeMap for sorted keys. Choose based on your needs!
